primary_diagnosis.name: Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure from retained secretions and mucus plugging, complicating Spinal Muscular Atrophy Type 1 (impending respiratory arrest)

comorbidities.name: Bulbar weakness with impaired swallow and airway protection

pathophysiology_summary: In SMA type 1, degeneration of anterior-horn motor neurons produces profound symmetrical flaccid weakness. The intercostal muscles are severely affected while the diaphragm is relatively spared, so the infant breathes almost entirely diaphragmatically — hence the bell-shaped chest and paradoxical (see-saw) breathing. Weak intercostals and weak bulbar muscles mean the cough is ineffective, so a trivial viral upper-respiratory illness produces secretions the infant physically cannot clear. Retained secretions cause mucus plugging, atelectasis and progressive ventilation-perfusion mismatch; combined with the increased work of breathing on a fixed, weak respiratory pump, the infant fatigues toward hypercapnic (type 2) respiratory failure and arrest. The problem is MECHANICAL failure of the respiratory pump plus failure of secretion clearance, not primarily infection or bronchospasm — so the treatment is mechanical airway clearance (cough-assist / insufflation-exsufflation) and non-invasive ventilatory support, not bronchodilators or (in the first instance) antibiotics.

differential.diagnosis: Acute respiratory decompensation of SMA with retained secretions / mucus plugging
differential.evidence_basis: Rapid respiratory deterioration after a mild viral cold in an infant with known severe neuromuscular weakness and an ineffective cough.

Could co-exist, but only a low-grade temperature and widespread (not focal) transmitted sounds; the dominant problem is mechanical secretion retention. Cannot be excluded remotely — needs hospital imaging/bloods.

differential.diagnosis: Bronchiolitis / lower respiratory tract viral infection
differential.evidence_basis: Viral prodrome fits, but in an SMA infant the lethal element is the inability to clear secretions, which reframes any viral LRTI as an emergency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor must treat this as a time-critical emergency in a neuromuscular infant, not a routine 'chesty cold'.

differential.diagnosis: Impending hypercapnic respiratory failure from neuromuscular pump failure + secretion retention
differential.evidence_basis: See-saw paradoxical breathing, weak ineffective cough, feeding refusal, exhaustion and borderline hypoxia (~91%) in SMA type 1.
No hepatomegaly or peripheral oedema; distress is mechanically explained by intercostal weakness.
2-day viral prodrome with widespread wet secretions rather than sudden localised stridor/wheeze.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnostic overshadowing is the key error to avoid — the new deterioration is an emergency, not baseline SMA.

differential.diagnosis: Acute respiratory failure / impending arrest from mucus plugging in SMA type 1
differential.evidence_basis: Mechanical failure to clear viral secretions on a weak respiratory pump; requires immediate mechanical airway clearance and PICU-level non-invasive ventilation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive disposition is emergency ambulance to a PICU; the AI Doctor cannot deliver the definitive care remotely.

red_flags.description: Paradoxical (see-saw) breathing with severe tachypnoea and chest-wall recession in an infant reliant on diaphragmatic breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sign of a failing respiratory pump — the infant is at the limit of compensation and heading for fatigue and hypercapnic failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition delays escalation; the infant progresses to respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Ineffective (weak) cough with retained wet secretions and audible upper-airway gurgling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secretion retention with no clearance mechanism guarantees rapid mucus plugging and atelectasis in SMA.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mucus plugging causes acute obstruction and fatal desaturation.

red_flags.description: Total feeding refusal with weak suck/swallow — bulbar involvement and aspiration risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks bulbar decompensation, dehydration risk and high aspiration risk; oral intake must stop.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aspiration of feed worsens airway compromise; dehydration accelerates deterioration.

red_flags.description: Exhaustion in an infant with borderline hypoxia (~91% on a home oximeter)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fatigue is the pre-terminal sign in paediatric respiratory distress — quiet exhaustion precedes arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Being reassured by an 'calmer' but exhausted infant leads to catastrophic under-triage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the new deterioration to 'just the SMA' or the infant's known floppiness, rather than recognising an acute, treatable, time-critical respiratory emergency.
Treating a 'chesty cold' as self-limiting; in SMA the inability to clear secretions turns a trivial virus into a life-threatening event.
Interpreting a tiring, quieter infant as improving; in paediatric respiratory failure, exhaustion is a pre-arrest sign.

Prescribing a bronchodilator or antibiotic as the answer — the problem is mechanical secretion retention and pump failure, needing cough-assist and non-invasive ventilation, not first-line pharmacology.
Attempting to coach prolonged home management instead of escalating; the AI cannot examine, measure a blood gas, or provide ventilatory support — 000 is mandatory.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultated air entry, rhonchi and degree of atelectasis at the lung bases
Exact blood pressure (85/50 mmHg) and precise heart rate (165 bpm)
Formal neurological examination confirming absent deep tendon reflexes and tongue fasciculations
Arterial/capillary blood gas (degree of hypercapnia) and chest imaging
Objective work-of-breathing and true degree of respiratory reserve
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 emergency ambulance to a Paediatric Intensive Care Unit. While waiting: keep the infant upright/positioned per the SMA care plan, do NOT feed by mouth, and coach the parents to use their OWN home mechanical cough-assist device per their existing care-plan settings if they are trained to; keep the infant on the home oximeter and stay with 000 on the line. Bring the cough-assist machine and the SMA care plan to hospital.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the syndrome (a known SMA infant deteriorating after a cold); the real risk is UNDER-action — treating it as a routine chest cold, or being falsely reassured by an exhausted, quieter infant, and failing to escalate.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Consensus Standard of Care for SMA (respiratory management) — Finkel et al. 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Proactive airway clearance (mechanical insufflation-exsufflation) and non-invasive ventilation during intercurrent respiratory illness; low threshold for hospital/PICU escalation in SMA type 1.
